title: The idlBNs package
text: Bayesian networks multivariate models assume joint probability distribution factorizes according acyclic directed graph (DAG). DAG structure Bayesian network can estimated data, -called problem structure learning. idlBNs package implements algorithms learning structure Bayesian networks observational interventional Gaussian data.

title: The idlBNs package
text: Structure learning algorithm Bayesian networks can classified three main categories: constraint-based, score-based, hybrid algorithms. idlBNs package implements score-based algorithms attempt following inclusion path search space DAGs (Kočka et al. 2001). Following inclusion path confers makes algorithm consistent faithfulness assumption, , converges generative structure sample size grows large Castelo Kočka (2003). Inclusion-driven learning Bayesian networks, idlBNs short1, can implemented either space canonical elements Markov equivalence classes DAGs, also known essential graphs, Greedy Equivalence Search (GES) algorithm (Chickering 2002), space DAGs, Hill-Climber Monte Carlo (HCMC) algorithm (Kočka Castelo 2001; Castelo Kočka 2003). GES algorithm adapted interventional data Greedy Interventional Equivalence Search (GIES) algorithm (Hauser Bühlmann 2012), efficient implementation available pcalg package (Kalisch Bühlman 2007), functions ges() gies(), respectively. idlBNs provides implementation R HCMC algorithm observational Gaussian data, interventional HCMC (iHCMC) algorithm mixture observational interventional Gaussian data (Castelo 2026) function hcmc().

what: Scoring functions for observational and interventional Gaussian data
title: The idlBNs package
text: idlBNs package implements two different functions scoring DAG structure observational interventional Gaussian data. iBIC() function, implements interventional Bayesian Information Criterion (iBIC) score (Hauser Bühlmann 2012, 2015), iBGe() function, implements interventional Bayesian Gaussian equivalent (iBGe) score (Kuipers Moffa 2025).

what: How to use the idlBNs package
title: The idlBNs package
text: illustrate use idlBNs package multivariate Gaussian data, simulated using procedures pcalg package purpose. Besides package, idlBNs package currently depends also Bioconductor package graph building representing DAGs object class graphNEL. start simulating Gaussian Bayesian network 10 vertices 50% density using function r.gauss.pardag() pcalg package, extracting DAG strcture converting essential graph using function dag2essgraph() pcalg package. Assume want consider two different singleton intervention targets (k=2k=2), .e., select two vertices DAG structure uniformly random intervene associated random variables, intervene one random variable time given multivariate observation. Following terminology Hauser Bühlmann (2012), define family intervention targets ℐ={I1,I2,I3}\mathcal{} = \{I_1, I_2, I_3\}, I1={∅}I_1=\{\emptyset\} Ii{vi}I_i\{v_i\} ={2,3}=\{2, 3\} vi∈Vv_i\V randomly selected vertex DAG structure G=(V,E)G=(V, E). Consider simulating n=100n=100 multivariate observations, similar number intervention target. Simulate mixed observational interventional multivariate Gaussian data DAG structure GG family intervention targets ℐ\mathcal{} using function rmvnorm.ivent() pcalg package, storing index corresponding intervention target row data. Run iHCMC algorithm informing presence interventional data. default, iBIC score used search algorithm. Calculate structural Hamming distance (SHD) essential graph generative DAG structure GG. Repeat search using iBGe score instead iBIC score. can see iBGe score leads estimate DAG structure close generative DAG structure, terms SHD. Now use HCMC algorithm without informing presence interventional data, .e., assuming data observational. setting, using either BIC BGe score leads worse estimates DAG structure.
code: library(graph) library(pcalg) library(idlBNs) p <- 5 d <- 0.6 set.seed(123) M_G <- r.gauss.pardag(p, d, top.sort=TRUE, normalize=TRUE) G <- as(M_G, "graphNEL") E <- dag2essgraph(as(G, "graphNEL")) k <- 2 I <- c(list(integer(0)), sample(p, size=k, replace=FALSE)) I [[1]] integer(0)  [[2]] [1] 5  [[3]] [1] 3 n <- 30 nbytgts <- rep(floor(n / (k + 1)), k) nbytgts <- c(n - sum(nbytgts), nbytgts) nbytgts [1] 10 10 10 dat <- list() for (v in seq_along(I)) {   targets <- I[[v]]   dat[[v]] <- rmvnorm.ivent(nbytgts[v], M_G, target=targets,                             target.value=rep(2, length(targets))) } dat <- do.call("rbind", dat) tindex <- rep(1:length(nbytgts), nbytgts) Dhat.ihcmc <- hcmc(dat, targets=I, target.index=tindex) Dhat.ihcmc $dag A graphNEL graph with directed edges Number of Nodes = 5  Number of Edges = 4   $sco [1] -50.0166 shd(dag2essgraph(Dhat.ihcmc$dag), E) [1] 3 Dhat.ihcmc.ibge <- hcmc(dat, targets=I, target.index=tindex, scorefun=iBGe) shd(dag2essgraph(Dhat.ihcmc.ibge$dag), E) [1] 2 Dhat.hcmc <- hcmc(dat) shd(dag2essgraph(Dhat.hcmc$dag), E) [1] 5 Dhat.hcmc.ibge <- hcmc(dat, scorefun=iBGe) shd(dag2essgraph(Dhat.hcmc.ibge$dag), E) [1] 4

title: Inclusion-Driven Learning of Bayesian Networks
text: idlBNs package implements inclusion-driven algorithms learning structure Bayesian networks. currently provides hill-climber Monte Carlo (HCMC) algorithm observational Gaussian data, interventional HCMC (iHCMC) interventional Gaussian data.

what: The HCMC and iHCMC algorithms — hcmc
title: The HCMC and iHCMC algorithms — hcmc
text: Run hill-climber Monte Carlo (HCMC) algorithm (Castelo Kočka, 2003) purely observational Gaussian data, interventional HCMC (iHCMC) mixed observational interventional Gaussian data (Castelo, 2026).

title: The HCMC and iHCMC algorithms — hcmc
code: hcmc(   dat,   r = 20,   targets = list(integer(0)),   target.index = rep(1L, nrow(dat)),   scorefun = iBIC,   MAXTRIALS = 5,   verbose = TRUE )

title: The HCMC and iHCMC algorithms — hcmc
text: dat data.frame matrix object, containing input Gaussian data, data value records rows random variables columns. r (Default 20) Maximum number (-)covered arc reversals. targets (Default list empty integer vector) Family intervention targets provided list integer vectors. default value implies interventions data purely observational. target.index (Default empty integer vector) vector integers one--one correspondence rows dat, indicating rows input data intervened targets. scorefun (Default iBIC) function calculate goodness fit (GoF) score DAG given data set. MAXTRIALS (Default 5) Maximum number trials escape local maxima. verbose (Default TRUE) Show progress calculations.

title: The HCMC and iHCMC algorithms — hcmc
text: list containing graphNEL object structure learned DAG, corresponding score.

title: The HCMC and iHCMC algorithms — hcmc
text: Castelo, R. Kočka, T. inclusion-driven learning Bayesian networks. Journal Machine Learning Research, 4:527-574, 2003. Castelo, R. Interventional idlBNs DAG-space. Challenges Algorithms Knowledge Discovery Data, M. van Leeuwen J.  Vreeken (eds.). LNCS 16067, Festschrift, Springer, 2026.

title: The HCMC and iHCMC algorithms — hcmc
code: suppressPackageStartupMessages({   library(graph)   library(pcalg)   library(idlBNs) })  p <- 5 k <- 2 n <- 30  ## simulate a random DAG set.seed(123) Mg <- r.gauss.pardag(p, 0.6, top.sort=TRUE, normalize=TRUE) g <- as(Mg, "graphNEL") e <- dag2essgraph(as(g, "graphNEL"))  ## generate a random family of intervention targets I <- c(list(integer(0)), sample(p, size=k, replace=FALSE))  ## sample size per different target (including the no-target) nbytgts <- rep(floor(n / (k + 1)), k) nbytgts <- c(n - sum(nbytgts), nbytgts)  ## simulate mixed observational and interventional data dat <- list() for (v in seq_along(I)) {   targets <- I[[v]]   dat[[v]] <- rmvnorm.ivent(nbytgts[v], Mg, target=targets,                             target.value=rep(2, length(targets))) } dat <- do.call("rbind", dat)  ## store the target index for each row of the data tindex <- rep(1:length(nbytgts), nbytgts)  ## run the HCMC algorithm assuming all data were observational dhat.hcmc <- hcmc(dat) #> ⠙ Score -87.004214222737 Escapes 0 Trials 0 #> ✔ Score -83.2933160642061 Escapes 0 Trials 0 [401ms] #>  dhat.hcmc #> $dag #> A graphNEL graph with directed edges #> Number of Nodes = 5  #> Number of Edges = 2  #>  #> $sco #> [1] -83.29332 #>   ## calculate the structural Hamming distance (SHD) between the generative ## DAG and the estimated DAG shd(e, dag2essgraph(dhat.hcmc$dag)) #> [1] 5  ## run the iHCMC algorithm informing the presence of interventional data ## using by the default the interventional BIC score (see the iBIC() ## function). dhat.ihcmc <- hcmc(dat, targets=I, target.index=tindex) #> ⠙ Score -59.3908043567074 Escapes 0 Trials 0 #> ⠹ Score -50.0166049861733 Escapes 0 Trials 0 #> ✔ Score -50.0166049861733 Escapes 0 Trials 0 [478ms] #>  dhat.ihcmc #> $dag #> A graphNEL graph with directed edges #> Number of Nodes = 5  #> Number of Edges = 4  #>  #> $sco #> [1] -50.0166 #>   ## the estimated DAG is closer to the generative DAG (lower SHD value) ## than the one estimated by HCMC, which did not take into account the ## presence of interventional data shd(e, dag2essgraph(dhat.ihcmc$dag)) #> [1] 3  ## run it again this time using the interventional BGe score (see the ## iBGe() function), which provides an estimate closer to the generative DAG dhat.ihcmc2 <- hcmc(dat, targets=I, target.index=tindex, scorefun=iBGe) #> ⠙ Score -189.956899563879 Escapes 0 Trials 0 #> ✔ Score -181.640790427507 Escapes 0 Trials 0 [524ms] #>  shd(e, dag2essgraph(dhat.ihcmc2$dag)) #> [1] 2

title: Straightforward (classical) hill-climbing algorithm — hillclimbing
text: Learn structure Bayesian network observational data using straightforward (classical) hill-climbing algorithm step search adds, removes reverses possible arcs.

title: Straightforward (classical) hill-climbing algorithm — hillclimbing
Straightforward (classical) hill-climbing algorithm — hillclimbing
text: dat data.frame object data records rows. scorefun (Default iBIC) function calculate goodness fit (GoF) score DAG given data set. verbose (Default TRUE) Show progress calculations.

title: Straightforward (classical) hill-climbing algorithm — hillclimbing
text: list containing graphNEL object structure learned DAG, corresponding score.

what: BGe score for interventional Gaussian data — iBGe
title: BGe score for interventional Gaussian data — iBGe
text: Score goodness--fit (GoF) given structure Bayesian network given interventional data set continuous values, observations assumed independent identically distributed (iid) multivariate Gaussian. GoF score corresponds interventional Bayesian Gaussian equivalent (iBGe) score defined Kuipers Moffa (2022). default, arguments targets target.index set calculated BIC score assumes interventions data.

title: BGe score for interventional Gaussian data — iBGe
code: iBGe(g, dat, targets = list(0L), target.index = rep(1L, nrow(dat)))

title: BGe score for interventional Gaussian data — iBGe
text: g acyclic directed graph (DAG) structure Bayesian network want calculate score. dat data.frame object data records rows. targets list object family targets. target.index vector integer values one--one correspondence rows dat, indicating set targets generated row dat.

title: BGe score for interventional Gaussian data — iBGe
text: single numeric value corresponding interventional BGe score given structure Bayesian network given data set.

title: BGe score for interventional Gaussian data — iBGe
text: Kuipers, J. Moffa, G. interventional Bayesian Gaussian equivalent score Bayesian causal inference unknown soft interventions. Proceedings Fourth Conference Causal Learning Reasoning (PMLR), 275:772-791, 2025.

title: BGe score for interventional Gaussian data — iBGe
code: library(graph)  p <- 3 nobs <- 100 nint <- 100 n <- nobs + nint  ## define a DAG structure of a Bayesian network with three vertices ## forming a Markov chain X1 -> X2 -> X3 g <- new("graphNEL", nodes=c("X1", "X2", "X3"), edgemode="directed") g <- addEdge("X1", "X2", g) g <- addEdge("X2", "X3", g)  ## simulate observational data for the previous DAG X1 -> X2 -> X3 set.seed(123) X1 <- rnorm(nobs, mean=0, sd=1) X2 <- 0.5 * X1 + rnorm(nobs, mean=0, sd=1) X3 <- 0.5 * X2 + rnorm(nobs, mean=0, sd=1) obsdat <- data.frame(X1=X1, X2=X2, X3=X3)  ## simulate interventional data for the same DAG, where X2 is intervened X1 <- rnorm(nint, mean=0, sd=1) X2 <- rnorm(nint, mean=0, sd=1) + 1.0 X3 <- 0.5 * X2 + rnorm(nint, mean=0, sd=1) intdat <- data.frame(X1=X1, X2=X2, X3=X3)  ## combine observational and interventional data dat <- rbind(obsdat, intdat)  ## define the targets and target indices for the interventional data targets <- list(0L, 2L) target.index <- c(rep(1L, nobs), rep(2L, nint))  ## calculate the interventional BGe score for the DAG and data set iBGe(g, dat, targets, target.index) #> [1] -713.708  ## create another Markov equivalent DAG by reversing the arc X1 -> X2 ## to obtain X1 <- X2 -> X3 g2 <- g g2 <- removeEdge("X1", "X2", g2) g2 <- addEdge("X2", "X1", g2)  ## calculate the interventional BGe score for the new DAG on the ## same data, notice that the score is different despite being a ## Markov equivalent DAG iBGe(g2, dat, targets, target.index) #> [1] -721.2087  ## this is not the case if we do not indicate the presence of interventions ## in the data iBGe(g, dat) #> [1] -891.4243 iBGe(g2, dat) #> [1] -891.4243

what: BIC score for observational and interventional Gaussian data — iBIC
title: BIC score for observational and interventional Gaussian data — iBIC
text: Score goodness--fit (GoF) given structure Bayesian network given interventional data set continuous values, observations assumed independent identically distributed (iid) multivariate Gaussian. GoF score corresponds Bayesian information criterion (BIC) implemented GaussL0penIntScore class pcalg package (Kalisch et al., 2012). default, arguments targets target.index set calculated BIC score assumes interventions data.

title: BIC score for observational and interventional Gaussian data — iBIC
code: iBIC(g, dat, targets = list(0L), target.index = rep(1L, nrow(dat)))

title: BIC score for observational and interventional Gaussian data — iBIC
text: g acyclic directed graph (DAG) structure Bayesian network want calculate score. dat data.frame object data records rows. targets list object family targets. target.index vector integer values one--one correspondence rows dat, indicating set targets generated row dat.

title: BIC score for observational and interventional Gaussian data — iBIC
text: single numeric value corresponding interventional BIC score given structure Bayesian network given data set.

title: BIC score for observational and interventional Gaussian data — iBIC
text: Hauser, . Buehlmann, P. Jointly interventional observational data: estimation interventional Markov equivalence classes directed acyclic graphs. Journal Royal Statistical Society Series B: Statistical Methodology, 77:291-318, 2015. Kalisch, M., Maechler, M., Colombo, D., Maathuis M.H. Buehlmann, P. Causal inference using graphical models R package pcalg. Journal Statistical Software, 47:1-26, 2012.

title: BIC score for observational and interventional Gaussian data — iBIC
code: library(graph)  p <- 3 nobs <- 100 nint <- 100 n <- nobs + nint  ## define a DAG structure of a Bayesian network with three vertices ## forming a Markov chain X1 -> X2 -> X3 g <- new("graphNEL", nodes=c("X1", "X2", "X3"), edgemode="directed") g <- addEdge("X1", "X2", g) g <- addEdge("X2", "X3", g)  ## simulate observational data for the previous DAG X1 -> X2 -> X3 set.seed(123) X1 <- rnorm(nobs, mean=0, sd=1) X2 <- 0.5 * X1 + rnorm(nobs, mean=0, sd=1) X3 <- 0.5 * X2 + rnorm(nobs, mean=0, sd=1) obsdat <- data.frame(X1=X1, X2=X2, X3=X3)  ## simulate interventional data for the same DAG, where X2 is intervened X1 <- rnorm(nint, mean=0, sd=1) X2 <- rnorm(nint, mean=0, sd=1) + 1.0 X3 <- 0.5 * X2 + rnorm(nint, mean=0, sd=1) intdat <- data.frame(X1=X1, X2=X2, X3=X3)  ## combine observational and interventional data dat <- rbind(obsdat, intdat)  ## define the targets and target indices for the interventional data targets <- list(0L, 2L) target.index <- c(rep(1L, nobs), rep(2L, nint))  ## calculate the interventional BIC score for the DAG and data set iBIC(g, dat, targets, target.index) #> [1] -242.3883  ## create another Markov equivalent DAG by reversing the arc X1 -> X2 ## to obtain X1 <- X2 -> X3 g2 <- g g2 <- removeEdge("X1", "X2", g2) g2 <- addEdge("X2", "X1", g2)  ## calculate the interventional BIC score for the new DAG on the ## same data, notice that the score is different despite being a ## Markov equivalent DAG iBIC(g2, dat, targets, target.index) #> [1] -249.149  ## this is not the case if we do not indicate the presence of interventions ## in the data iBIC(g, dat) #> [1] -326.3833 iBIC(g2, dat) #> [1] -326.3833

what: idlBNs: Inclusion-driven learning of Bayesian networks — idlBNs-package
title: idlBNs: Inclusion-driven learning of Bayesian networks — idlBNs-package
text: idlBNs package implements inclusion-driven algorithms learning structure Bayesian networks. currently provides hill-climber Monte Carlo (HCMC) algorithm observational Gaussian data, interventional HCMC (iHCMC) interventional Gaussian data.

title: idlBNs: Inclusion-driven learning of Bayesian networks — idlBNs-package
text: main functions : iBIC() - interventional BIC score Gaussian data. iBGe() - interventional BGe score Gaussian data. hillclimbing() - straightforward hill-climbing algorithm learning structure Bayesian networks. hcmc() - HCMC iHCMC algorithms learning structure Bayesian networks observational interventional data, respectively. detailed information usage, see package vignette, typing vignette("idlBNs"). code development version package available GitHub repository https://github.com/rcastelo/idlBNs. Questions bug reports posted opening issue idlBNs GitHub repo https://github.com/rcastelo/idlBNs/issues.

title: idlBNs: Inclusion-driven learning of Bayesian networks — idlBNs-package
text: Maintainer: Robert Castelo robert.castelo@upf.edu Authors: Robert Castelo robert.castelo@upf.edu
